text.title: AIDA Landing Page Structure: Section-by-Section Rules
text.summary: Rules for each AIDA section: hero, problem agitation, solution/benefits, how-it-works, social proof, FAQ, and final CTA.

rule.statement: Hero must include: headline (6-12 words, benefit not feature), subheadline (expands with specificity), product visual, one CTA button, and a social proof line ("Trusted by X users").
rule.rationale: Visitors decide in 5 seconds whether to scroll. Every missing element reduces the probability they continue. The social proof line directly below the CTA button increases CVR by 10-15%.

rule.statement: Headline formula: [Outcome] + [Timeframe] + [Without Pain Point]. Write 10 variations before selecting the final one.
rule.rationale: The first headline written is rarely the best. Forcing 10 variations surfaces options that use more specific language and stronger benefit framing.

example.note: Headline: "Build your SaaS in 2 weeks without writing backend code" — outcome (build SaaS), timeframe (2 weeks), without pain (no backend code).
Headline: "The AI-Powered Platform for Modern Developers" — no outcome, no timeframe, no pain addressed. This describes what the product is, not what it does for the user.

rule.statement: Testimonial formula: "[Specific result achieved] in [timeframe]. Before [product], I struggled with [problem]. Now [positive outcome]." — [Name], [Title] at [Company].
rule.rationale: Generic testimonials ("Great product!") add no conversion value. Specific results with numbers and named individuals are credible and benchmarkable.

rule.statement: Social proof effectiveness order: customer logos (B2B) > testimonials with photos > case studies > numbers ("50K users") > press mentions > ratings.
rule.rationale: Logos borrow institutional credibility. Named testimonials borrow personal credibility. Anonymous numbers are least credible because they cannot be verified.

antipattern.description: Agent-polished testimonials with fabricated specifics (company names, exact numbers not from the original quote).
antipattern.reason: Fabricated testimonials are FTC violations and destroy trust when discovered. Agent should format and polish real quotes, never invent specifics.

rule.statement: FAQ must address conversion objections, not product questions. "Can I cancel anytime?" and "Is my data secure?" convert better than "How do I import my data?"
rule.rationale: At the FAQ stage, visitors are close to converting but have unresolved concerns. Answering usage questions serves existing users; answering objections serves prospects.

section.title: Final CTA

rule.statement: Repeat the main CTA with: benefit reminder, risk reversal (30-day money-back, no credit card, free trial), and urgency if appropriate.
rule.rationale: Visitors who scroll to the bottom are high-intent but may have lingering hesitation. Risk reversal removes the last friction at the decision moment.

section.reference: Full SaaS landing page section order with placeholder content for all AIDA elements
Conversion checklist to score above-fold, copy, trust, and technical elements
